reasoning:
The question asks to predict the probability that the daily mean temperature at Troyes-Barberey on three future dates (Nov 2, Nov 25, Jan 24) will be strictly higher than on 2025-10-26. We are forecasting based on climatology since no short-term weather data for 2025-10-26 is available (most recent is Oct 16 at 12.738 °C, which is near average). Climatologically, the mean temperature in Troyes declines from late October to late January. 
1. Early November (Nov 2) mean is ~8.8 °C compared to late October (Oct 26) mean of ~10.5 °C, a difference of -1.7 °C. Assuming independent variances of daily anomalies (~13 for each), the std of the difference is ~5.0 °C. The probability that T(Nov 2) > T(Oct 26) is P(Z > 1.7 / 5.0) ≈ 37%. 
2. Late November (Nov 25) mean is ~5.3 °C (difference of -5.2 °C). Probability is P(Z > 5.2 / 5.1) ≈ 16%. 
3. Late January (Jan 24) mean is ~3.3 °C (difference is -7.2 °C). Probability is P(Z > 7.2 / 5.3) ≈ 9-11%. 

I am relying strictly on climatological base rates and normal distribution difference approximations to estimate the chance of an anomalously warm winter day beating an anomalously cold late-autumn day. Though the prompt provides a generic instruction that "uncertainty should decrease / move closer to 0.5" for distant dates, physical seasonality dictates otherwise: the expected temperature difference grows starkly more negative in the distant future, overriding the simple expansion of forecast uncertainty space. I will output 0.37, 0.18, and 0.11.
